coredata.dc:title: Gold@Carbon Quantum Dots Nanocomposites Based Two-In-One Sensor: A Novel Approach for Sensitive Detection of Aminoglycosides Antibiotics in Food Samples
coredata.dc:creator: Sajwan, Reena K.
Solanki, Pratima R.
coredata.dc:description: Antibiotics are life-saving drugs for humans, but their unwanted use leads to antibacterial resistance (ABR) and causes serious health problems. The excess of these antibiotics entered to the food chain and caused food contamination. Here, Au@CQDs nanocomposites (NCs) was used as a two-in-one sensor to detect two antibiotics. The color change of AuNCs and fluorescence resonance energy transfer are two distance-dependent phenomena used as sensing mechanisms. In the sensing process, Au@CQDs NCs change their color, enhancing the fluorescence intensity of NCs in the presence of Gentamicin (GENTA) and Kanamycin (KMC) antibiotics. The limit of detection of 116 nM and 133 nM for GENTA and 195 nM and 120 nM for KMC have been achieved with colorimetric and fluorimetric readout, respectively. The practicality of the reported sensor was evaluated in real spiked samples and showed excellent recovery efficiency. Therefore this two-in-one sensor can be used for the food monitoring system.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au@CQDs Nanocomposites
Aminoglycosides
Gentamicin
Kanamycin
Colorimetric
Fluorimetric
Food Samples

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Au@CQDs nanocomposites
Sample preparation for the detection of antibiotics
Optical properties and surface morphology and particle size analysis
Influence of Au salt in the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs and sensing mechanism of antibiotics
Two-in-one sensing of antibiotics and selectivity analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: For the completion of the present research work, the authors are very much thankful to Advanced Instrumentation & Research Facility (AIRF) JNU, New Delhi, for providing a TEM and TRFS characterization facility. The authors are also grateful o the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India (PAC-SCNS-PS-ICMR-1293) for providing financial support and motivation.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Absorbance and FL spectra of CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs, inset image shows visual emission of CQDs under UV-lamp exposure. (B) FL spectra at varying excitation wavelengths from 300 − 430 nm. (C) FTIR spectra of CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs, and (D) XRD analysis of Au@CQDs NCs.

TEM images of (A) CQDs, inset image shows the particle size distribution of CQDs. (B) Au@CQDs NCs; the inset image shows the particle size distribution. (C) Accumulation of Au@CQDs NCs in the presence of AGCs antibiotics, and (D) particle size distribution of Au@CQDs NCs after the addition of antibiotics.

(A) Absorbance spectrum of Au salt and Au@CQDs NCs. (B) FL intensity spectra of Au@CQDs NCs and the inset image show quenching efficiency with different concentrations of Au salt. (C) The Naked eyes visualization of Au@CQDs NC with different Au salt concentrations (1 to 9 as 0.0, 0.062. 0.12, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mg/mL) in the presence of daylight and (D) in UV-light.

(A) Spectral overlapping between emission and absorption spectra of CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs, respectively. (B) Life-time decay of CQDs before and after the formation of Au@CQDs NCs. (C) Absorption spectra and (D) recovery in the FL intensity of Au@CQDs with GENTA-1000 nM (curve b) and KMC-1000 nM (curve c) antibiotics, respectively. (E) Au@CQDs NCs based colorimetric detection of GENTA and (F) KMC (200 nM- 2000 nM).

(A) Absorption spectra of Au@CQDs NCs with GENTA and (C) KMC. (B) The calibration curve of A766/A535 versus antibiotics GENTA and (D) KMC concentration with an inset linear response curve. (E) The recovery in the FL intensity of the FRET pairs in the presence of GENTA and (G) KMC. (F) The calibration curve (recovery efficiency versus varying concentration of GENTA and (H) KMC with inset shows their relationship.
Systematic representation of two-in-one sensing probe for antibiotics.

head.ce:title: Gold@Carbon Quantum Dots Nanocomposites Based Two-In-One Sensor: A Novel Approach for Sensitive Detection of Aminoglycosides Antibiotics in Food Samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano-bio Laboratory, Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano-bio Laboratory, Special Center for Nanoscience, Jawaharlal Nehru University, New Delhi 110067, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Antibiotics are life-saving drugs for humans, but their unwanted use leads to antibacterial resistance (ABR) and causes serious health problems. The excess of these antibiotics entered to the food chain and caused food contamination. Here, Au@CQDs nanocomposites (NCs) was used as a two-in-one sensor to detect two antibiotics. The color change of AuNCs and fluorescence resonance energy transfer are two distance-dependent phenomena used as sensing mechanisms. In the sensing process, Au@CQDs NCs change their color, enhancing the fluorescence intensity of NCs in the presence of Gentamicin (GENTA) and Kanamycin (KMC) antibiotics. The limit of detection of 116 nM and 133 nM for GENTA and 195 nM and 120 nM for KMC have been achieved with colorimetric and fluorimetric readout, respectively. The practicality of the reported sensor was evaluated in real spiked samples and showed excellent recovery efficiency. Therefore this two-in-one sensor can be used for the food monitoring system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are used as a magic bullet that precisely attacks microbes that are responsible for disease causations, but at the same time, they would not affect the host (Page & Gautier, 2012). The uncontrolled use of antibiotics caused a significant threat to human health by developing antibacterial resistance (ABR) (Acar & Rostel, 2001). In recent years, India has become a worldwide hotspot for ABR, and common bacteria developed resistance and increased it further, which is responsible for the number of treatment failures (Organization, 2014). ABR infections can be complicated and sometimes impossible to treat and lead to increases in the number of patients and economic costs associated with the healthcare system. Besides, the human health sector's other concern in India is the extensive use of antibiotics in the food-animal production sectors (<Tolerance_Limit_Antibiotics_01_08_2018.Pdf>, n.d.). More than 700,000 people have lost their lives due to ABR, and another 10 million will be on the edge of dying from it by 2050. ABR kills more people than cancer, and road accidents combined with uncontrolled utilization of antibiotics, mainly common in poultry production. In the world's meat production, India comes in 5th position and produced around 7 million tons in 2015–16, of which 54 % belongs to red meat only, and the remaining is from poultry meat (Sivaraman & Parady, 2018). India is also the world's giant milk, and 3rd largest egg producer after USA and China, and the fourth-largest chicken producer after the USA, Brazil, and China. In India, chicken is one of the most consumed meat because it is cheaper than other meats (Sivaraman & Parady, 2018).

Among all the antibiotics, the aminoglycosides (AGCs) class is the most widely used veterinary medicine in all food-producing animals to treat bacterial infections (Zhang et al., 2021). The World Health Organisation classifies these antibiotics as a critically essential human medicine. Gentamicin (GENTA) and Kanamycin (KMC) antibiotics are effective against rapidly growing bacteria and are highly used in humans and veterinarians to treat infections caused by gram-positive and negative bacteria. These antibiotics are most widely used to treat pneumonia, urinary tract infection, soft tissue infection, mastitis, sepsis, and other infections (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022). These antibiotics' dosages are associated with severe side effects such as loss of hearing, toxicity to the kidney, respiratory failure, allergic reaction, and ABR (Wang et al., 2017). The excess amount of antibiotics that enter the food chain or are excreted through urine, contaminate the environment causing a significant threat to human health and environmental safety worldwide (Properties, 2022). Therefore, it is essential to trace antibiotic residue in food samples for the future well-being of humans and animals. According to India's Food safety and standard authority, the food's antibiotic residue should not exceed the maximum residue limit of 0.001 mg/kg (<Tolerance_Limit_Antibiotics_01_08_2018.Pdf>, n.d.).

In the current era, instrumental techniques are used for detecting antibiotic residue in food, inspiring traditional analytical methods. Liquid chromatography, in addition to mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) (Haller et al., 2002), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Van den Meersche et al., 2016), and gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy (GS- MS) (Yang et al., 2015), enzyme-linked immunosorbent (ELISA) (Jin et al., 2005), and electrochemical analysis methods are most widely used for the detection of residue level of antibiotics in food samples. These methods are sensitive but are time-consuming and expensive; they require expertise and special reagents and lack rapidity for practical clinical use; that's why it's not to be used as spot detection of antibiotics. Rapid detection of antibiotics followed by effective antibiotic treatment can potentially decrease the emergence of ABR. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop fast and selective methods with low-cost instrumentation to detect AGCs antibiotics which nanomaterials-based optical techniques can achieve.

The optical technique is a powerful detection tool capable of providing analyte information remotely, multimodal detection compared to other biosensors that use electronic (Allen et al., 2007), electrochemical (Wang, 2005), or mechanical transduction (Carrascosa et al., 2006). It is preferred in chemical and biological applications. Colorimetric and fluorimetric based optical methods have easy setup and operations and are more convenient and user-friendly. The colorimetric readout system has the advantage that the visualization of color change can be seen through the naked eye and helps in the qualitative analysis of analytes in test samples. Apart from this, the fluorescence (FL) based method is more widely used for developing fluorimetric biosensors due to their user-friendly operation, simple setup, and adorable sensitivity. The detection mechanism is mainly based on the quenching or enhancing the FL intensity of fluorescent nanomaterials to detect antibiotics. The sensing mechanism such as static quenching, dynamic quenching, inner filter effect (Sajwan et al., 2021), photo-induced electron transfer (Sajwan et al., 2021), and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) is defined as well-known mechanisms behind the FL principle (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022).

Among the FL analyses, the FRET system holds high sensitivity and selectivity; this has attracted significant attention. FRET is a distance-dependent phenomenon between two molecules. It occurs through the non-radiative process in which excited state donor (fluorophore) transfer their energy to nearby acceptor molecules through long-range dipole–dipole interaction between donor–acceptor pair (Amjadi et al., 2015). For the FRET process, the system should have to satisfy-two criteria: (i) the absorption spectrum of the acceptor must overlap with the FL intensity spectra of the donor; (ii) the distance between the donor–acceptor pair should be in the close vicinity (<10 nm) (Hussain, 2009). FRET is tremendously used as a spectroscopic technique in all applications of FL, with medical diagnostics, optical imaging, nucleic acid detection (Lu et al., 2009), immunoassay (Zhang et al., 2012), antibiotic detection (Zhang et al., 2016), metal ion detection (Mansell et al., 2008), pesticides detection (Yuan et al., 2017), glucose (Hu et al., 2012), and pH sensor (Aslan et al., 2005). In usual FRET systems, fluorescent dyes are generally used as a donor and an acceptor. Such dye-based FRET systems usually suffer photo-bleaching, interference, and poor stability in ambient environments (Amjadi et al., 2015). Researchers have developed different nanomaterial-based FRET systems in the past few years to overcome this problem. For that, fluorescent nanomaterials like semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) (Tao et al., 2015), upconversion nanoparticles (Li et al., 2014), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Amjadi et al., 2015) were used as donors and acceptors.

CQDs are a novel class of carbon nanomaterials with quasi-spherical particles below 10 nm has prominent FL properties (Wang et al., 2017). CQDs gained considerable attention over other semiconductor QDs due to their remarkable FL properties, photo-stability, biocompatibility, highly water soluble, and low toxicity (Wang & Hu, 2014). The abundance of carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino surface groups of CQDs provided viability for surface modification. CQDs can be used as fluorometric sensors for detecting various analytes, which it uses as donors and acceptors (Lim et al., 2015). These surface groups facilitate CQDs as reducing agents for synthesizing Au, Ag, and Pd nanoparticles (NPs) and nanocomposites (NCs) (Liu et al., 2014). So far, chemical reduction methods have been widely used to synthesize metal nanoparticles. Metal NPs (Au and Ag) have excellent antibacterial properties and exhibit FL properties due to the recombination of free charge carriers generated from the absorption of excitation photons. The visible region emission of these metal NPs originated due to the electronic transition between the sp-d band and enhanced the emission intensity when it interacted with localized surface plasmon (Marinescu et al., 2020; Motelica et al., 2021).

Gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) are used to develop color-based sensors for detecting GENTA (Gukowsky et al., 2018) and as an energy acceptor for constructing a donor–acceptor detection system. Sodium borohydride and tri-sodium citrate were used as chemical-reducing agents to synthesize AuNPs. CQDs can be the reducing agent for synthesizing gold nanocomposites (AuNCs). It has the advantage over the conventional synthesis process, such as reducing the synthesis time, and synthesis takes place at room temperature. Most importantly, forming Au@CQDs NCs does not require a reducing agent (Hu et al., 2019). Au@CQDs NCs have more outstanding properties rather than individual CQDs and AuNPs. In Au@CQDs NCs, Au acts as an excellent energy acceptor, and CQDs act as energy donors forming an energy donor–acceptor pair. This FRET pair is used as a colorimetric and fluorimetric system that effectively and sensitively detects antibiotics in food samples.

In current work, Au@CQDs NCs based two-in-one colorimetric and fluorimetric “Off-On” sensor has been developed for detecting GENTA and KMC antibiotics. Here, CQDs were used as Au salt-reducing agents to form Au@CQDs NCs. The sensing mechanism behind the detection of antibiotics was based on two distance-dependent phenomena: the color change of Au@CQDs NCs and the FRET phenomenon. The FL intensity of CQDs decreased after the formation of Au@CQDs NCs due to the energy transfer that takes place between them. When GENTA and KMC were added separately to Au@CQDs NCs, NCs got agglomerated and changed their color from wine red to grayish blue with the increasing amount of antibiotics. The aggregated NCs settle down after the centrifugation and diminish the energy transfer efficiency between AuNCs and CQDs, resulting in the enhancement of FL intensity of Au@CQDs NCs. The two-in-one system can detect lower concentrations of GENTA and KMC in the test sample as 116 nM and 195 nM with colorimetric, respectively, and 133 nM and 120 nM with fluorimetric, respectively. The lowest value of antibiotics detected by the sensor with colorimetric mode was found as 382.8 nM (GENTA) and 643.5 nM (KMC), and in the fluorimetric readout system, 438.9 nM (GENTA) and 396.0 nM (KMC). The practicality of the sensor was checked in actual food samples, and it verified that the proposed method shows an excellent response to GENTA and KMC antibiotics and can be potentially used in food monitoring applications.
Materials and instrumentation details were provided in the Supplementary data files.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and Au@CQDs nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared with the pyrolysis method, in which citric acid (CA) was taken as a carbon source. For that, 2 g of CA spread evenly in a 50 mL beaker and then put in the hot plate at a temperature of 200 °C. At this temperature, CA melted completely and converted into a transparent solution within 3 min; after that, it continued for another 30 min. At the end of the reaction time, the transparent solution changed into deeper brown color, indicating the formation of CQDs. The reaction beaker is removed from the hot plate and then allowed to cool to room temperature (27 °C). At room temperature, the CQDs were collected in 30 mL DI water and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm to remove the larger particles. The supernatant was taken and stored at 4 °C for future use.

For the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs, CQDs can be used as the reducing agent because they have plenty of carboxylic moieties on their surface and can help to synthesize Au@CQDs NCs without the addition of any other reducing agents. Au@CQDs NCs synthesis was done by preparing Au salt solution (2.0 mg/mL) in DI added with the fixed amount of CQDs. For this, 100 μL of prepared CQDs were added to 200 μL DI and 50 μL of 2.0 mg/mL Au salt solution as shown in Scheme S1(Supplementary data) and kept at room temperature. The color of the reaction solution turns yellow to light pink and wine red within 20–25 min of incubation time. The red wine color of the solution indicated the formation of NCs while using CQDs as a reducing agent. The prepared Au@CQDs NCs were stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sample preparation for the detection of antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: In a two-in-one antibiotic detection system, varying concentrations of GENTA and KMC were prepared as (200, 300, 400, 500, 600, 700, 800, 900, 1000, 1200, 1400, 1600, 1800, and 2000 nM) in DI. For the colorimetric detection of GENTA and KMC, 500 μL of Au@CQDs NCs was taken in a 1 mL centrifugation tube, and added 100 μL of different concentrations of two antibiotics, which were further incubated for 20 min. After that, absorption spectra of the reaction solution were recorded using a spectrophotometer. For fluorimetric detection, the same reaction sample was taken in a series of 1 mL centrifugation tubes and further incubated for 20 min. After that, the reaction solution was centrifuged for 4 min at 5500 rpm. The supernatant was collected and recorded the FL spectra of the reaction solution at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. All the experimental conditions kept the same throughout the experiments.

Selectivity analysis: The selectivity and practicality of the two-in-one sensor were tested in real food samples. For that, other familiar antibiotics, metal ions, and metabolites present in food samples were taken for the selectivity study. The interferent concentrations were taken as 5, 10, and 50 μM for antibiotics, metal ions, and metabolites, respectively. The inference analysis was taken out under optimized experimental conditions.

Real Samples: The effectiveness of the sensor and the presence of antibiotics were quantified in natural milk and egg samples. Milk and egg samples were purchased from Munirka, a local market at New Delhi, India. Before the test, the milk sample was pretreated, and the excess amount of proteins and fat contained was removed. For that, the milk sample was diluted 20-fold and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to separate the protein and fat; after centrifugation, the middle part of the samples was collected in a new centrifuge tube followed by the addition of methanol (400 μL in 1 mL diluted milk sample) to precipitate the protein and then centrifuged again for 15 min at the same rpm. The supernatant was collected and then tested against GENTA and KMC. When no traces of antibiotics were found in the real sample, the milk samples were spiked with two different concentrations of GENTA and KMC, and performed the colorimetric and fluorimetric analyses.
The egg sample preparation process was followed as reported in our previous work (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022). The egg samples testing procedure was similar to the milk samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and surface morphology and particle size analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of synthesized CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs were characterized with photo spectroscopy techniques. The absorbance and the FL spectra of CQDs and NCs were recorded with UV–vis and FL spectroscopy setups, as shown in Fig. 1 (A and B), respectively. In Fig. 1 (A), spectra (a) denoted the absorbance of CQDs having a characteristic peak at 280 nm and 312 nm corresponding to the π-π* and n-π* transition of CC aromatic bond and CO bond, respectively. The FL emission of CQDs mainly originated from the n-π* transition (Sajwan et al., 2021). CQDs (d) emission spectra show green–blue emission at 482 nm at an excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The inset image of Fig. 1(A) depicts the visual emission of CQDs under UV-lamp exposure. The absorbance peak at 535 nm confirms the formation of Au@CQDs NCs as shown in curve (b), and spectra of CQDs and NCs were shown in Figure S1. The absorption spectra of NCs satisfactorily overlapped with the FL spectra of CQDs and fulfilled the critical need for developing a FRET-based sensing probe.

Fig. 1(B) shows the green–blue emission of CQDs recorded with varying excitation wavelengths in the 300–430 nm range. The highest emission intensity was obtained at 482 nm at the excitation wavelength of 390 nm. The emission spectrum of CQDs shows the excitation-dependent behavior where the peak position of CQDs (482 nm) shifted towards the higher wavelength (red shift) with the increasing excitation wavelengths (300–430 nm). The excitation-dependent emission behavior is mainly observed due to the surface defects and variation in the particle size distribution of CQDs (Sarkar et al., 2018).

The functional group compositions of CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs were characterized through FTIR spectroscopy, and the spectrum is shown in Fig. 1(C). The FTIR spectra of CQDs and NCs (curve a and b) show an IR peak at 3554 cm−1 depicting the OH stretching. The peaks around 2909 and 2976 cm−1 represent the anti-symmetric stretching of the CH bond present in CA (Sajwan et al., 2021). The presence of the OH and CO groups on the CQDs surface was confirmed with FTIR and corresponding peaks obtained at 1717 and 1408 cm−1, respectively (Sarkar et al., 2018). The CO stretching vibration in the carboxylic group is depicted through the IR peak at 1058 cm−1. The existence of carboxylic groups on CQDs and NCs surface makes it highly hydrophilic, having negative zeta potential ζ = -31.15 mV and ζ = -30.65 mV, respectively, and confirms the formation of NCs.

The phase, crystallinity, and structure of Au@CQDs NCs were analyzed with the XRD characteristics, as shown in Fig. 1(D). The XRD patterns represented four major diffraction peaks that appeared at 2θ values 38.04°, 44.19°, 64.44°, and 77.43° concerning the (111), (200), (220), and (311) planes, respectively confirming the face-centered cubic (FCC) structure of AuNPs regarding JCPDS file no. 04–0784 (Cheng et al., 2015). The broadening of the XRD peak at 26.47° corresponding to the (002) plane confirms the presence of CQDs in Au@CQDs NCs. The crystalline size of AuNPs was calculated using the Scherrer formula in equation (1)
(1)
D
n m
=
K λ
β
c o s θ

In the equation, D shows crystallite size; K is a constant (∼0.9), λ (1.54 Å) is X-ray wavelength, β is full-width half maxima (FWHM), and θ is the Bragg's diffraction angle (Yadav et al., 2020). The average crystallite size was calculated as 14.67 nm corresponding to the peak of the (111) plane.

The surface morphology of CQDs and Au@CQDs NCs were studied with the help of TEM. TEM images and particle size distribution of CQDs and Au@CQDs NC are shown in Fig. 2 . TEM images of CQDs depicted the well dispersed semi- spherical particles with an average size of 3.88 ± 0.58 nm. Fig. 2(A) inset image shows that the particle size distribution varies from 2.0 to 5.5 nm. The NCs were prepared by reducing Au salt with the help of as-prepared CQDs. Fig. 2(B) shows the TEM image of NCs with an average particle size of around 19.16 ± 2.6 nm having wide size distribution that varies from 12.21 to 33.77 nm, as presented in the inset of Fig. 2(B).

Further, Au@CQDs NCs were used as a colorimetric and fluorimetric probe for the detection of AGCs antibiotics. In the presence of antibiotics, NCs get agglomerated and change their color from wine red to grayish blue. The aggregation of NCs increased the particle size of NCs up to 29.98 ± 4.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 2(C). Fig. 2(D) shows that the particle size distribution of NCs varies from 15.47 to 49.50 nm after adding antibiotics. It was observed that the particles came to closure and increased their size.

The elemental analysis of Au@CQDs NCs was done with the help of Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX)-SEM, and obtained images are shown in Figure S2. The spherical surface morphology was established in image S2 (A) with the scanning area of 500 nm scale. In the SEM images of elemental analysis (shown in images S2 (B)), images 1, 2, 3, and 4, indicate the presence of elements such as carbon (C), oxygen (O), silicon (Si), and gold (Au), respectively presented in NCs. The presence of Si elements occurs due to the preparation of the sample on a silicon wafer. The elemental mapping confirms the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of Au salt in the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs and sensing mechanism of antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs, Au salt concentrations plays an essential role in the formation of NCs in the presence of a fixed value of CQDs. Fig. 3 (A) shows the absorption spectra, which confirm the formation of NCs with varying concentrations of Au salt 0.062. 0.12, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 8.0 mg/mL in the presence of the fixed amount of CQDs. According to the absorption of the NCs, with the increasing concentration of Au salt, the formation of NCs was observed through the color change from light yellow to wine red, as shown in Fig. 3(C).

Similarly, Fig. 3(B) depicts the FL intensity of CQDs by increasing salt concentrations. It was observed that the intensity of CQDs decreased as long as NCs formed with the increasing concentrations of Au salt. The decrease in the FL intensity with a rising concentrations of Au salt was visually observed under the exposure of the UV lamp, as shown in Fig. 3(D). It was observed that the acceptor significantly absorbed the FL intensity of the donor. As a result, the FL intensity of CQDs gets quenched after the formation of NCs due to the FRET phenomenon occurring between CQDs and NCs. The decrease in the FL intensity of CQDs in the presence of AuNCs at different Au salt concentrations, shown in the inset of Fig. 3(B), and the quenching intensity was calculated with the following equation.
(2)
F
Q u e n c h i n g
=
F
C Q D s
-
F
A u @ C Q D s
F
C Q D s
where FCQDs and FAu@CQDs are the FL intensity of CQDs before and after the formation of NCs.

Sensing Mechanism: In the developed sensor, CQDs can work as a reducing agent for the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs as well as a donor for the construction of FRET-pair. AuNCs can widely be used as fluorescent acceptors due to their high absorption constant value and broader absorption spectrum in the visible region (Sajwan et al., 2021). Fig. 4 (A) shows the spectral overlapping between the absorbance spectra of NCs and the FL spectra of CQDs, which justifies the significant criteria for constructing FRET- pairs. According to the FRET phenomena, the energy transfer takes place from donor to acceptor only when the absorption spectra of the acceptor molecule adequately overlap with the emission spectra of the donor molecule. In this work, NCs plays dual role as a donor and acceptor. Fig. 3 (B) shows that the FL intensity of CQDs gets absorbed by NCs when excited at 390 nm. To confirm the energy transfer phenomenon, the FL life-time decay of CQDs and NCs was analyzed with TCSPC measurement, and result represented in Fig. 4(B). The averaged life-time decay of CQDs with and without the presence of an acceptor was calculated using the following equation (Singh et al., 2022).
(3)
τ
¯
=
a 1
τ
1
2
+
a 2
τ
2
2
a 1
τ 1
+
a 2
τ 2
where,
τ
¯
is the average life-time decay of the donor with and without the addition of the acceptor, τ1 and τ2 are the first and second exponential decay time, respectively, a1, and a2 is the amplitude corresponding to τ1 and τ2.

According to Eq. (3), the calculated life-time decay of CQDs is observed as 2.83 nsec (curve a), whereas this life-time decay was found to be decreased by 2.22 nsec (curve b) due to the formation of Au@CQDs NCs. The decrease in the life-time after the synthesis of NCs confirmed that the energy transfer takes place between the donor and acceptor; therefore, the FL intensity of CQDs decreased.

The NCs were used further as a two-in-one colorimetric and fluorimetric system for the detection of two antibiotics. In Au@CQDs NCs, CQDS act as donor, whereas AuNCs can be used as an acceptor and a colorimetric indicator. The sensing mechanism is most prominently dependent on two distance-dependent phenomena: a color change of NCs in addition to antibiotics; and the energy transfer process between CQDs and AuNCs, which reduced the FL intensity of CQDs. Fig. 4(C) shows the absorption spectra of (a) Au@CQDs NCs, (b) Au@CQDs + GENTA (1000 nM), and (c) Au@CQDs + KMC (1000 nM). Further, by adding the antibiotics into the colloidal solution of AuNCs, it gets agglomerated due to the formation of Au-N bond with an amine group present in the molecular structure of antibiotics (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022). The absorption spectrum of agglomerated NCs shows a second peak at 766 nm, represented in the curves (b and c) of Fig. 4 (C). The color of NCs changed from wine red to grayish blue with varying concentrations of two antibiotics, as shown in Fig. 4 (E and F), respectively, and the increased averaged particle size of NCs up to 29.98 ± 4.5 nm as represented in Fig. 2 (D).

In fluorimetric-based sensing, the FL intensity of CQDs initially decreased due to the energy transfer after forming Au@CQDs NCs (curve a). Then in the presence of antibiotics, the recovery in the FL intensity of CQDs was observed, as shown in curves (b and c) in Fig. 4 (D). The aggregation of NCs reduced the FRET efficiency between CQDs and NCs and enhanced the FL intensity of CQDs.
Au@CQDs NCs-based two-in-one sensor could be the most straightforward way for qualitative/colorimetric and quantitative/fluorimetric detection of two antibiotics.

Scheme 1 represents a two-in-one colorimetric and fluorimetric distance-dependent sensing mechanism for antibiotics. When Au salt is added to CQDs, they form Au@CQDs NCs and quenched their FL intensity due to the energy transfer, as shown in curve (b) in section (I). Section (II) represented the colorimetric and fluorimetric ways of detection. For colorimetric-based detection, AuNCs get agglomerated in the presence of antibiotics. They change their color from wine red to grayish blue, observed due to the proximity between NCs and antibiotics. The color change can be visually seen through the naked eye and helps qualitative determination of antibiotics in the test samples. Due to the accumulation, the absorption peak of NCs shifted to the higher wavelength in the presence of GENTA and KMC, as shown in curves (b) and (c), respectively, in graph (B) of section (II).

In fluorimetric-based detection, the FL intensity of CQDs gets absorbed by the AuNCs due to their energy transfer. Hence, the FL intensity of CQDs was quenched after the formation of NCs, as shown in curve (a) in the graph (C). In the presence of antibiotics, NCs get agglomerated, and their signature absorption peak shifts to a higher wavelength. As a result, the spectral overlapping decreased to some extent, increased the distance between donor and acceptor, and reduced the FRET efficiency.

In section (II), curves (b) and (c) of the graph (C) represented the FL intensity of NCs in addition to GENTA and KMC, respectively. The FL recovery efficiency of NCs is proportional to the concentration of antibiotics, and it could be calculated with the help of the following formula.
(4)
R e c o v e r y
E f f i c i e n c y
η r
=
F -
F
0
F 0
where F and F0 represented the FL intensity of NCs with and without the addition of antibiotics, and ηr presented the recovery efficiency of the developed sensor.

All the experimental parameters that influenced the sensor's performance should be checked and analyzed before the sensing applications to improve the sensor's performance. In investigating the significant parameters like the time and pH value required for the synthesis of NCs, these parameters and incubation time also be examined, which plays an essential role in sensing.

Synthesis Time: For the synthesis of Au@CQDs NCs, Au salt was added to the optimized amount of CQDs and kept at room temperature from 0 to 20 min of the time interval for the formation of NCs. The absorption spectra and the FL intensity of NCs were timely recorded with UV–vis and FL spectroscopy. Figure S3 (A and B) directed the complete formation of NCs in 20 min of the time interval. The visualization of the color change from light yellow to wine red was shown in Figure S3 (C), confirming the formation of NCs. The quenching in the FL intensity of NCs with time was represented in Figure S3 (B) inset and calculated with equation (2).

pH Value: The pH of the solution is an important parameter. It plays an essential role in the synthesis of NCs and the recovery efficiency of the developed sensor. To investigate the pH value NCs were synthesized in different pH values from 4.0 to 8.0, and corresponding absorption and FL spectra were recorded and depicted in Figure S4 (A and B). This study used CQDs as a reducing agent for the synthesis of AuNCs, as they have plenty of carboxylic groups on their surface. The protonation and deprotonation of CQDs occur at acidic and basic pH (Sajwan & Solanki, 2022; Song et al., 2016), which affects the formation of NCs. The day and UV light visualization of NCs at different pH value have shown in Figure S4 (C and D). The Inset of Figure S4 (A and B) represents the maximum absorption and the FL quenching at 6.8 (pH of DI).

Incubation Time: The colorimetric and fluorimetric probes used NCs to detect antibiotics. Figure S5 (A and B) shows the aggregation state of NCs in the presence of GENTA and KMC, respectively. With the variation of their incubation time, the absorption spectra show a new peak appeared at a higher wavelength at 766 nm. In the presence of antibiotics, the absorbance spectra of NCs recorded with a time ranging from 0 to 20 min. The aggregation process of NCs takes 20 min to complete. The inset of Figure S5 (A and B) shows the ratio between the absorption at 766 nm (A766) and 535 nm (A535), representing the agglomeration state of NCs with the variation of incubation time.

Hence, after investigating all the necessary parameters that could affect the performance of the developed sensors, it concluded that the synthesis of the NCs takes 20 min at solution pH 6.8 and 20 min of incubation time required for the complete interaction between NCs and antibiotics. Throughout the experiment, kept all the parameters were the same.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Two-in-one sensing of antibiotics and selectivity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: After parameters optimization, a NCs-based two-in-one probe was used to detect antibiotics in test samples, as shown in Fig. 5 . With the addition of rising concentrations of antibiotics, the NCs get agglomerated and change their color from wine red to grayish blue, as presented in Fig. 4(E and F). The absorption spectra of NCs in the presence of antibiotics (shown in Fig. 5(A and C)) could be used to determine antibiotics qualitatively.

In addition to different concentration of two antibiotics, the aggregation of AuNCs takes place, due to which a new absorption peak at 766 nm occurred in the absorption spectra resulting the aggregation state of AuNCs (Gukowsky et al., 2018). The absorption peak intensity of AuNCs at 535 nm decreased and simultaneously, the peak intensity at 766 increased with varying concentrations of antibiotics, as shown in Fig. 5 (A and C). The calibration curve shown in Fig. 5(B and D) represented the aggregation state of AuNCs in the presence of GENTA and KMC, respectively. The aggregation state was analyzed by the ratio between the absorption at 766 nm (A766) and 535 nm (A535). The increased ratio value indicates the disparity of AuNCs in the presence of antibiotics. When the concentration of antibiotics increased from 200 − 2000 nM, the ratio of (A766/A535) also increased, indicating the aggregation of AuNCs as shown in Fig. 5 (B and D). The inset of Fig. 5 (B and D) shows the linear relationship between the state of aggregation and the concentration of antibiotics with the lower value of detection (LOD) 116 nM and 195 nM for GENTA and KMC, respectively. The limit of quantification (LOQ) is the lowest value of the antibiotics that the system can detect. Here, LOQ has been calculated as 382.8 nM and 643.5 nM in the case of GENTA and KMC, respectively.

The following equation calculated the LOD and the LOQ.
(5)
L O D =
3 σ
s
(6)
L O Q = 3.3 ∗
L O D
where σ denotes the standard deviation of the response of the curve and s is the slope of the calibration curve.

In colorimetric detection, NCs agglomerated in addition to antibiotics and increased the particle size. The increased particle size was settled down by centrifugation, which deceased the energy transfer between the FRET pairs and resulted in the recovery of FL intensity of CQDs with increasing concentration of antibiotics, as shown in Fig. 5 (E) in the case of GENTA (300 – 1800 nM) and (G) for KMC (300–1400 nM). It was noticed that the presence of antibiotics agglomerate AuNCs and their absorption spectra shifted toward the higher wavelength, which significantly reduced FRET efficiency and increased the recovery efficiency of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5 (F and H) in the case of GENTA and KMC, respectively. The Inset of Fig. 5 (F and H) represents the linear relationship between recovery efficiency and the concentration of GENTA and KMC.

The FRET-based sensor shows recovery efficiency of 63.27 % and 70.68 % in the case of GENTA and KMC, respectively. The LOD and LOQ of the fluorimetric system were calculated as 133 nM, 438.9 nM, and 120 nM, 396.0 nM, in the case of GENTA and KMC, respectively.

Table S1 compares the present work based on all the earlier reported systems developed for detecting GENTA and KMC with the two-in-one system. The detection range and the LOD of the developed system were found as good and as comparable to the other reported methods. Apart from the detection range, the present way has the advantage of an easy and well-defined sensing mechanism. In this work, CQDs play a dual role, such as reducing agents for the synthesis of AuNCs and donors for and construction of FRET pair, which lowers the workload and cost of the system. Using NCs as a two-in-one sensor for detecting more than one antibiotic makes it more adorable and affordable for detecting AGCs class of antibiotics. Compared to the previous method, the developed system acts as a novel system that fulfills all the sensing requirements like simplicity, sensitivity, and affordability.

Selectivity Analysis: The selectivity study of the developed sensor was carried out in the presence of different interferents present in milk and egg real samples. The majorly found interferents are antibiotics, metal ions, and other metabolites. Therefore, these interferents are used to determine the selectivity of the sensor. The interferent concentrations were selected by their physiological range, and the sensing range, such as metabolites (50 μM), metal ions (10 μM), and other antibiotics (5 μM), were used. The absorption ratio and recovery efficiency of Au@CQDs NCs in the presence of all the interferents and with GENTA and KMC antibiotics were individually recorded to evaluate the sensor's selectivity. The result was summarized in Figures S6 (A and B). So, the developed sensor results showed high selectivity towards AGCs antibiotics.

Real Sample Analysis: After the evolution of all the testing parameters interferents, selectivity, and sensitivity study, the developed sensor's applicability and practicality were tested in real samples like milk and egg. For that, real samples were directly added to the sensing probe to monitor the presence of antibiotics. The negative result indicated that there were no traces of antibiotics in real samples, then spiked the real samples with two known standard concentrations of GENTA (600 nM and 1200 nM) and KMC (700 nM and 1200 nM) as listed in table S2 and S3 for the practicality check of the system. The spiked samples show excellent recovery varying from 80.37 % to 98.93 % with good relative standard deviation (RSD) vales 3.04 % to 5.49 % in GENTA and 83.93 % to 99.52 % with RSD 3.87 % to 7.78 % in the case of KMC in the two-in-one mode of detection. The real sample analysis concluded that the developed sensor is suitable for antibiotic detection in real sample applications.

In conclusion, a novel Au@CQDs NCs-based two-in-one sensor was developed to detect GENTA and KMC. The simple synthesis approaches were adopted, in which CQDs were used as a reducing agent to form Au@CQDs NCs. The sensing mechanism is based on two distance-dependent phenomena: FRET and the color-changing properties of AuNCs. The developed methods showcased their simplicity and easy route of nanomaterials synthesis and used them without further surface implementation, making the developed system less laborious and affordable. The developed sensor worked in the detection range of 200 – 2000 nM. It showed a recovery efficiency of 63.27 % with LOD 133 nM and LOQ 438.9 nM for GENTA, were 70.68 % recovery with LOD 120 nM and LOQ 396.0 nM for KMC in fluorimetric readout mode. In the colorimetric readout mode, the sensor can detect lower concentrations of GENTA in test samples as 116 nM and for KMC as 195 nM with LOQ 382.8 nM and 643.5 nM, respectively. The developed sensors showed excellent recovery while tested with spiked milk and egg samples and could be suitable for food safety applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: For the completion of the present research work, the authors are very much thankful to Advanced Instrumentation & Research Facility (AIRF) JNU, New Delhi, for providing a TEM and TRFS characterization facility. The authors are also grateful o the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), India (PAC-SCNS-PS-ICMR-1293) for providing financial support and motivation.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.135590.

ce:section.ce:para:

Chemistry–A European Journal

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, C., Xu, J., Zhang, S., Ji, X., & He, Z. (2012). One-Pot Synthesized DNA–CdTe Quantum Dots Applied in a Biosensor for the Detection of Sequence-Specific Oligonucleotides. Chemistry–A European Journal, 18(27), 8296–8300.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aminoglycoside Resistance and possible mechanisms in Campylobacter spp. Isolated from chicken and swine in Jiangsu, China
